title: Resonance Proliferation Across Localization Transitions
authors: Carlo Vanoni, David M. Long, Anushya Chandran
abstract: Models of many-body localization (MBL) exhibit slow numerical drifts towards delocalization with increasing system size, for which no satisfactory theory exists. Numerics indicates that these drifts are driven by the proliferation of many-body resonances at intermediate disorder strengths. We develop a statistical method to predict the distribution of resonance oscillation frequencies which captures how the formation of resonances at larger frequency scales subsequently affects the formation of resonances at lower frequencies. Working within the statistical Jacobi approximation (SJA), we derive a flow equation for a power-law exponent $θ(w)$ characterizing the density of resonances at frequency scale $w$. A localized phase is described by a line of fixed points with $θ(w)>0$, while an instability of the flow signals resonance proliferation and the onset of thermalization. The predicted $θ(w)$ matches numerics on the Anderson model on random regular graphs and the Lévy-Rosenzweig-Porter random matrix ensemble, both of which host resonance-driven delocalization transitions. We further connect the flow to eigenstate properties such as the participation ratio and to dynamical observables such as the return probability. The predicted $θ(w)$ also matches what is numerically measured in real-space models of MBL at intermediate disorder strengths, representing a significant step towards explaining the finite-size drifts observed in MBL.
summary: The paper proposes that the observed numerical drifts in Many-Body Localization are driven by the proliferation of many-body resonances. By developing a statistical method based on the Jacobi algorithm, the authors derive flow equations that describe how resonances accumulate to trigger a transition to thermalization, matching numerical results across several disordered models.
main_problem: Understanding the physical mechanism behind the slow numerical drifts toward delocalization in Many-Body Localization (MBL) models and explaining how resonance proliferation drives these transitions.
main_result: The authors derive a flow equation for a scale-dependent resonance exponent theta(w) that predicts the onset of thermalization via resonance proliferation, identifying the transition as belonging to the BKT universality class.
method: The Statistical Jacobi Approximation (SJA) is used to track the evolution of Hamiltonian matrix elements during an iterative Jacobi diagonalization process, leading to the derivation of functional flow equations.
model_or_system: The study covers several models including the Anderson model on random regular graphs, the Levy-Rosenzweig-Porter random matrix ensemble, and real-space MBL models like the disordered Heisenberg spin chain.
key_observables: Resonance exponent theta(w), participation ratio (PR), return probability, and the density of resonances.
important_parameters: Frequency scale (w), disorder strength (W), resonance exponent (theta), and the critical scale (wc).
main_assumptions: The SJA assumes a power-law ansatz for the distribution of matrix elements and neglects correlations that build up during the decimation process.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Jacobi algorithm procedure, the evolution of the resonance exponent theta(w) across different models, and the connection between resonance density and eigenstate participation ratios.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MBL drifts, develops the SJA framework and flow equations, applies the theory to various random matrix and graph models, and finally connects the results to real-space MBL dynamics and observables.
why_interesting: This paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding the stability of localized phases and the mechanism of thermalization through resonance proliferation, which is central to many-body dynamics and the study of prethermal regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic representation of two of the models we study using the Jacobi algorithm, namely the Anderson model on random regular graphs (top left) and a disordered quantum spin chain (bottom left). The right panel shows the action of the Jacobi algorithm on a matrix, with the gray scale representing the size of the matrix element. At each iteration, the current largest element, whose value is denoted by w and highlighted in red, is zeroed by a 2-level rotation. This rotation affects the rows and columns containing the element w (dashed red lines). FIG. 2. Numerical solution of the heuristic flow equations, Eq. (28), for f(θ) = 0.1(1 −θ). Different colors represent different initial conditions (w = 1, θ0), and the arrows indicate the direction of the flow. Blue curves represent the localized phase, while red curves represent the delocalized phase. The black dashed line is the separatrix, such that θ = 0 for w = 0.

FIG. 3. Sketch of the integrated number of resonances nres(w) (top row) and of θ(w) (bottom row) as predicted by the heuristic flow equation (left) and found in the numerical simulations (right). The arrows indicate the direction of flow-time and thicker lines on the right panel indicate the regions where the numerical results are in agreement with the flow equation predictions. The heuristic flow equation predicts that, in the ergodic phase (red), n(w) diverges at a finite value of w = wc and θ(w) →−∞as w →∞, while both quantities remain finite for all log(w) in the localized phase (blue). Numerically, the predicted divergences are not observed due to finite-size effects. Rather, θ(w)...

FIG. 4. Jacobi flow of θ(w) in the three models presented in Sec. IV A. The solid thin lines are the bare numerical data, and the shaded area around the curves represents the statistical error on θ, obtained using the bootstrapping method applied to the distribution of decimated elements and propagated to θ. The solid thick lines are obtained by applying a weak Gaussian filter to the numerical data to remove the fluctuations at large values of w; the thick lines are plotted until θ reaches its minimum value, i.e., before the flow enters the dense regime (see text and Fig. 3).
